sections.label: Situation Overview
sections.title: Two-Week Ceasefire Begins After Trump Threat
paragraphs: The United States and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire that took effect on 8 April local time after President Donald Trump threatened massive strikes on civilian infrastructure. The ceasefire came just hours before Trump's deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz or face attacks on power plants and bridges.

Iran agreed to allow safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz for two weeks, while the US suspended all offensive operations. Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, acting as intermediary, announced that peace talks will begin in Islamabad on Friday 10 April. However, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the ceasefire does not include Lebanon, contradicting Pakistan's claims.
key_facts: Ceasefire announced 7 April Washington time, implemented 8 April Middle East time
Iran to coordinate safe passage through Strait of Hormuz with armed forces
Peace talks scheduled for Islamabad on Friday 10 April
Israel says ceasefire excludes Lebanon operations
sections.australia_relevance: Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese expressed support for the ceasefire and praised international negotiators' efforts.

sections.label: Background
sections.title: War's Origins and Key Developments
paragraphs: The current conflict began on 28 February 2026 when the US and Israel launched airstrikes assassinating Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and other officials. Iran responded by closing the Strait of Hormuz and launching missile strikes against Israel, US bases, and allied countries in the region.

The war has killed over 3,400 people across the Middle East, including more than 1,900 in Iran from Israeli and US strikes. Iran had previously orchestrated attacks on Australian soil in 2024, leading Australia to expel Iran's ambassador and list the Revolutionary Guard Corps as a terrorist organisation.
key_facts: War began with assassination of Supreme Leader Khamenei on 28 February
Strait of Hormuz closed by Iran in March, disrupting 20% of global oil
Over 3,400 killed regionwide, 1,900+ in Iran
Iran previously attacked Australian synagogue in Melbourne
sections.australia_relevance: Three Australian Defence Force personnel were aboard the US submarine that sank an Iranian frigate, though PM Albanese said they took no offensive action.

sections.label: Diplomacy
sections.title: International Response and Sanctions
paragraphs: Trump faced criticism for saying US allies including Australia 'didn't help us' in the Iran war, despite Australia providing defensive support in the Gulf. Iran earlier rejected a US 45-day ceasefire proposal, demanding permanent peace and lifting of all sanctions.

Iran's 10-point proposal reportedly includes withdrawal of US forces from regional bases, lifting of all sanctions, compensation payments, and Iran's right to nuclear enrichment. China and Russia vetoed a UN Security Council resolution aimed at reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
key_facts: Trump criticised Australia and Japan for not helping in Iran conflict
Iran demands permanent peace, not temporary ceasefire
Iranian proposal includes lifting sanctions and US withdrawal
China-Russia veto blocked UN resolution on Strait reopening
sections.australia_relevance: Australia has deployed 85 personnel and E-7 Wedgetail aircraft to UAE for defensive operations, plus reportedly SAS forces for contingency planning.

sections.label: Economy & Energy
sections.title: Global Oil Crisis Hits Australian Consumers
paragraphs: US petrol prices have risen to a national average of $4.14 per gallon, up 39% since the war began. Oil prices surged past $114 per barrel before easing slightly after ceasefire news, with analysts warning prices could hit $185 per barrel if disruptions last three months.

In Australia, analysts predict petrol prices could jump 40 cents per litre, with some warning of $3.00 per litre if conflicts persist. The government has temporarily halved fuel taxes, helping prices drop 30 cents recently, while maintaining 39 days of petrol reserves and 29 days of diesel.
key_facts: US petrol up 39% to $4.14/gallon since war began
Oil peaked above $114/barrel, could hit $185 if disruptions persist
Australian petrol could rise 40c/litre, potentially reaching $3.00
Australia has 39 days petrol reserves, 29 days diesel
sections.australia_relevance: Australia imports 90% of liquid fuel and government has activated emergency fuel reserves while temporarily cutting fuel taxes to ease price pressure.

sections.label: What to Watch
sections.title: Fragile Ceasefire and Energy Markets
paragraphs: The two-week ceasefire faces immediate challenges, with Israel excluding Lebanon from the agreement despite Pakistan's broader interpretation. Iran has warned that any deviation from the agreement could lead to renewed violence, keeping military forces on high alert.

Energy markets remain volatile with the International Energy Agency calling this the 'largest supply disruption in oil market history'. Full restoration of Strait of Hormuz traffic will take months even if the ceasefire holds, keeping upward pressure on global fuel prices through 2026.
key_facts: Disagreement over whether ceasefire includes Lebanon
Iran warns of 'full force' response if agreement violated
IEA calls this largest oil supply disruption in history
Full Strait restoration expected to take months
sections.australia_relevance: Australian officials will meet with counterparts next week to devise plans for potential military operations to protect shipping if ceasefire fails.
